content: Can we rebase the rust branch on the upstream linux branch or rust-next branch periodically? so that our added previous code can be always run on the newest rust impl.

Now the rust branch is useful to see what is left to upstream, and for some downstream projects to base their work on top. If we rebase the rust branch, code what is left to upstream will still be working, and some downstream projects maybe/can keep pace with the rust linux head

Can we rebase the rust branch on the upstream linux branch or rust-next branch periodically?

If you mean merging Linus' tree to the rust branch, then I have done that 30+ times over the years :)

so that our added previous code can be always run on the newest rust impl.

Which added code? Do you mean a PR? Or code that you have elsewhere based in the rust branch?

Either way, please note that the rust branch has been obsolete for months now (please see https://rust-for-linux.com/branches#rust).

If you have somewhere code that relies on the Rust support in the kernel (rust or otherwise), then we would appreciate if you let us know what the use case is. If you have a user that is public and can be upstreamed to the kernel, then that is a great way to justify adding more Rust abstractions to the kernel.

Now the rust branch is useful to see what is left to upstream, and for some downstream projects to base their work on top. If we rebase the rust branch, code what is left to upstream will still be working, and some downstream projects maybe/can keep pace with the rust linux head

We understand that over time we got some downstream users, and we appreciate all the interest in the Rust support.

But please do not base work on top of rust. It is not supported, it contains APIs that have been reworked substantially since then (e.g. all types using pinned-init), it contains experimental/WIP code, etc.

And since some APIs have been reworked w.r.t. mainline, in order to keep your code working, we would need to keep all the old code/types/APIs as-is. And even if we did that, we would not know if we would have successfully kept your code working.

Miguel Ojeda said:

Can we rebase the rust branch on the upstream linux branch or rust-next branch periodically?

If you mean merging Linus' tree to the rust branch, then I have done that 30+ times over the years :)

----I mean rebase rust branch on the linux'tree or rust-next . which let some code which have not been upstreamed to linux-tree such as the rust/kernel/fs.rs code to be applied to the linux'tree head or rust-next head.

so that our added previous code can be always run on the newest rust impl.

Which added code? Do you mean a PR? Or code that you have elsewhere based in the rust branch?
-----added code: i mean the rust/kernel/fs.rs like code and some pr or the code that have elsewhere based in the rust branch you just mentioned.
Either way, please note that the rust branch has been obsolete for months now (please see https://rust-for-linux.com/branches#rust).
----I mean 'rust' branch is not the current 'rust' branch, i understand that current rust branch have been obsolete. I mean the 'rust' branch is the branch/or commit only from the init rust support(which have been upstreamed to linus tree to the commit/commits which have been in the rust branch such as the fs support but have been not upstreamed to the linus tree., and any after commits such as the commit synced from the upstream linus tree or the rust-next branch would be excluded.
If you have somewhere code that relies on the Rust support in the kernel (rust or otherwise), then we would appreciate if you let us know what the use case is. If you have a user that is public and can be upstreamed to the kernel, then that is a great way to justify adding more Rust abstractions to the kernel.

Now the rust branch is useful to see what is left to upstream, and for some downstream projects to base their work on top. If we rebase the rust branch, code what is left to upstream will still be working, and some downstream projects maybe/can keep pace with the rust linux head

We understand that over time we got some downstream users, and we appreciate all the interest in the Rust support.

But please do not base work on top of rust. It is not supported, it contains APIs that have been reworked substantially since then (e.g. all types using pinned-init), it contains experimental/WIP code, etc.
-----same reason. I mean the 'rust' branch is cleanly rust branch, which based linustree/rust-next , and extrally have the fs like commit which would be upstreamed to linustree/rust-next to but now just have been not upstreamed .

And since some APIs have been reworked w.r.t. mainline, in order to keep your code working, we would need to keep all the old code/types/APIs as-is. And even if we did that, we would not know if we would have successfully kept your code working.

----I mean 'rust' branch is not the current 'rust' branch, i understand that current rust branch have been obsolete. I mean the 'rust' branch is the branch/or commit only from the init rust support(which have been upstreamed to linus tree to the commit/commits which have been in the rust branch such as the fs support but have been not upstreamed to the linus tree., and any after commits such as the commit synced from the upstream linus tree or the rust-next branch would be excluded. If we just rebase our rust branch on the linustree or rust-next, then we will no need cherry-pick/sync commits from linux tree or new commit just match the linus tree's, such as the commit "rust: upgrade to Rust 1.71.0".

(Please format your messages properly, it is fairly hard to read otherwise.)

I mean rebase rust branch on the linux'tree or rust-next . which let some code which have not been upstreamed to linux-tree such as the rust/kernel/fs.rs code to be applied to the linux'tree head or rust-next head.

Rebasing would give you the same contents as the merges I performed in the past. Anyway, not all the code in rust is suitable or intended for upstreaming as it is, for different reasons.

If we just rebase our rust branch on the linustree or rust-next, then we will no need cherry-pick/sync commits from linux tree or new commit just match the linus tree's, such as the commit "rust: upgrade to Rust 1.71.0".

The rebasing you are asking for is the upstreaming effort that we are doing, for those use cases where we have a user. This involves cleaning things up, re-reviewing them to catch design/soundness/... issues if any, writing proper commit messages, splitting dependencies, talking to other kernel maintainers, and so on and so forth. It is not as simple as "just rebase".

Therefore, if you have a downstream user that you can submit upstream, then please let us know and we will try to help you getting your dependencies and your user there.

If you do not intend to upstream a user to the kernel, then please note that rust branch is not supported, that it is going away soon, and that some of its contents may never be upstreamed. That is why we need use cases in-tree: in order to upstream things.

Anyway, not all the code in rust is suitable or intended for upstreaming as it is, for different reasons.

so we should not rebase.

I just find the current workflow is complex, at least upstreame the rust branch's some code. But according your explanation, we cannot improve it anymore.

Maybe it's not obvious, but since Rust support is in mainline now, most of our interest is to upstream Rust bindings and drivers, so the workflow will be not different as other subsystems described in: https://docs.kernel.org/process/development-process.html .
let me first be familiar with kernel dev process.
